items.q: Why did William of Normandy claim the English throne in 1066?
items.correct: He claimed Edward the Confessor had promised him the throne in 1051 and that Harold Godwinson had sworn an oath to support that claim in 1064.
items.misconception: William invaded because he was the strongest king in Europe and wanted more land.
items.random_wrong: William was Edward the Confessor's eldest son.
items.why: Students treat the invasion as raw conquest, missing that William framed it as enforcing a legitimate hereditary and oath-based claim — legitimacy mattered hugely in 11th-century politics.

items.q: Why was Vesalius significant in the history of medicine?
items.correct: His 1543 De Humani Corporis Fabrica corrected Galen's errors via human dissection and accurate illustration, founding modern anatomy though not curing disease.
items.misconception: Vesalius discovered the cause of disease and ended humoral theory.
items.why: Vesalius improved anatomy but disease theory still ran on humours for centuries. Students conflate progress in one area (structure) with progress in another (cause of illness).

items.q: Why was Jenner's smallpox vaccination significant in 1796?
items.correct: He showed cowpox material could prevent smallpox safely — the first effective vaccine, though he did not know why it worked; germ theory came nearly a century later.
items.misconception: Jenner discovered germs and used them to make vaccines.
items.random_wrong: Jenner used penicillin to cure smallpox.
items.why: Jenner worked empirically, not from germ theory which was Pasteur's. Students collapse the discoveries together; vaccination predates the science explaining it.
The answer examiners want is: A 1916 soldier's letter. “A 2020 textbook on WWI” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does 'inference' mean in source work?
items.misconception: Copying a source word for word
items.why: Correct answer: Drawing a conclusion from clues. “Copying a source word for word” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many letters does the OPCVL framework have?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
